summary: Bryan Cantrill reflects on the discontinuation of USENIX ATC, a conference he once lionized but watched decline over two decades as it became overly academic and disconnected from practitioners. He argues that while the conference failed, systems innovation itself thrived through open source—with Go, Rust, and production systems flourishing outside traditional academic publishing. The conference model of publishing, combined with the inherent difficulty of in-person events, ultimately doomed ATC, though Cantrill acknowledges it was once a singular forum for pioneering systems work.
key_points: USENIX ATC declined because it became too academic and lost connection to industry practitioners
Open source became the new vector for systems innovation, enabling languages like Go and Rust that couldn't arise in a proprietary world
The conference model of publishing in computer science was fundamentally flawed compared to journals
In-person conferences face inherent challenges that online formats address more cost-effectively
Despite its decline, ATC at its height was a singular forum for pioneering systems work
money_quotes: Go and Rust do not arise—cannot arise—in a proprietary world. These are also not the work of hobbyists: they were written by professionals for professionals in a professional capacity.
Importantly, the vector for publishing for the practitioner is via repositories rather than being confined to only formal writing.
So USENIX ATC did finally succumb, and I do view it as a casualty here, asphyxiated by the rough love of academic computer science and its inability to grow beyond the conference model of publishing.
While they certainly lose something in the process, it feels like online conferences give us indisputably (overwhelmingly!) more bang for the buck.
ATC was—at its height—a singular forum for presenting pioneering systems work.
key_insight: Open source repositories have replaced academic conferences as the primary vector for systems innovation, enabling production-focused work that couldn't exist in a proprietary world while traditional conferences like USENIX ATC suffocated under academic publishing models.
